- Explore: Read-only search agent for broad fan-out searches — when answering means sweeping many files, directories, or naming conventions and you only need the conclusion, not the file dumps. - Plan: Software architect agent for designing implementation plans. Use this when you need to plan the implementation strategy for a task. - deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo, defaults to 10m) - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

# Crossbeam Channel

[![Build Status](https://github.com/crossbeam-rs/crossbeam/workflows/CI/badge.svg)](
https://github.com/crossbeam-rs/crossbeam/actions)
[![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/license-MIT_OR_Apache--2.0-blue.svg)](
https://github.com/crossbeam-rs/crossbeam/tree/HEAD/crossbeam-channel#license)
[![Cargo](https://img.shields.io/crates/v/crossbeam-channel.svg)](
https://crates.io/crates/crossbeam-channel)
[![Documentation](https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/badge.svg)](
https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel)
[![Rust 1.74+](https://img.shields.io/badge/rust-1.74+-lightgray.svg)](
https://www.rust-lang.org)
[![chat](https://img.shields.io/discord/569610676205781012.svg?logo=discord)](https://discord.com/invite/JXYwgWZ)

This crate provides multi-producer multi-consumer channels for message passing.
It is an alternative to [`std::sync::mpsc`] with more features and better performance.

Some highlights:

* [`Sender`]s and [`Receiver`]s can be cloned and shared among threads.
* Two main kinds of channels are [`bounded`] and [`unbounded`].
* Convenient extra channels like [`after`], [`never`], and [`tick`].
* The [`select!`] macro can block on multiple channel operations.
* [`Select`] can select over a dynamically built list of channel operations.
* Channels use locks very sparingly for maximum [performance](benchmarks).

[`std::sync::mpsc`]: https://doc.rust-lang.org/std/sync/mpsc/index.html
[`Sender`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/struct.Sender.html
[`Receiver`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/struct.Receiver.html
[`bounded`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/fn.bounded.html
[`unbounded`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/fn.unbounded.html
[`after`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/fn.after.html
[`never`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/fn.never.html
[`tick`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/fn.tick.html
[`select!`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/macro.select.html
[`Select`]: https://docs.rs/crossbeam-channel/latest/crossbeam_channel/struct.Select.html

## Usage

Add this to your `Cargo.toml`:

```toml
[dependencies]
crossbeam-channel = "0.5"
```

## Compatibility

Crossbeam Channel supports stable Rust releases going back at least six months,
and every time the minimum supported Rust version is increased, a new minor
version is released. Currently, the minimum supported Rust version is 1.74.

## License

Licensed under either of

 * Apache License, Version 2.0 ([LICENSE-APACHE](LICENSE-APACHE) or http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0)
 * MIT license ([LICENSE-MIT](LICENSE-MIT) or http://opensource.org/licenses/MIT)

at your option.

dual licensed as above, without any additional terms or conditions.

#### Third party software

This product includes copies and modifications of software developed by third parties:

* [examples/matching.rs](examples/matching.rs) includes
  [matching.go](http://www.nada.kth.se/~snilsson/concurrency/src/matching.go) by Stefan Nilsson,
  licensed under Creative Commons Attribution 3.0 Unported License.

* [tests/mpsc.rs](tests/mpsc.rs) includes modifications of code from The Rust Programming Language,
  licensed under the MIT License and the Apache License, Version 2.0.

* [tests/golang.rs](tests/golang.rs) is based on code from The Go Programming Language, licensed

See the source code files for more details.

[package]
name = "crossbeam-channel"

version = "0.5.15"

license = "MIT OR Apache-2.0"

<result># Crossbeam Channel Receiver-Only Flavors Analysis

## 1. `at.rs` - One-Time Timer Channel

### Core Struct(s) and Fields
- **`Channel`** (lines 19-25)
  - `delivery_time: Instant` - the instant when message will be delivered
  - `received: AtomicBool` - tracks whether message has been received
- **`AtToken`** (line 16): `type AtToken = Option<Instant>` - result of receive operation

### Lazy Message Generation on Receive
- **`try_recv`** (lines 39-59):
  1. Optimistic check if already received with `Relaxed` ordering (line 41)
  2. Check if `Instant::now() < delivery_time` (line 46)
  3. Atomically swap `received` flag with `SeqCst` ordering (line 52)
  4. Returns `delivery_time` as the message on success (line 54)

- **`recv`** (lines 63-98):
  1. Optimistic received check (line 65)
  2. Loop comparing current time against `delivery_time` and optional `deadline`
  3. Sleep using `thread::sleep(deadline - now)` (line 86)
  4. Atomically swap `received` flag with `SeqCst` (line 90)
  5. Returns `delivery_time` or blocks forever if already received (line 95)

### Select-Related Methods (lines 143-194)
- **`try_select`** (lines 145-157): calls `try_recv()`, sets `token.at = Some(msg)` on success
- **`deadline`** (lines 160-167): returns `Some(delivery_time)` if not received, `None` otherwise
- **`register`** (lines 170-172): returns `self.is_ready()`
- **`accept`** (lines 178-180): calls `try_select(token)`
- **`is_ready`** (lines 183-185): returns `!self.is_empty()`
- **`watch`** (lines 188-190): returns `self.is_ready()`

### Thread Parking/Context/Deadline Interaction
- Uses `thread::sleep(duration)` for blocking (line 86)
- Uses `utils::sleep_until(deadline)` for indefinite blocking after message consumed (line 95)
- Does NOT use Context-based thread parking

### Key Concurrency
- **`AtomicBool`** for the `received` flag
- **Relaxed ordering** for optimistic checks (lines 41, 65, 110, 162)
- **SeqCst ordering** for the critical swap operation (lines 52, 90, 121)

### Approximate Line Numbers
- Core struct: 19-25
- try_recv: 39-59
- recv: 63-98
- SelectHandle impl: 143-194

---

## 2. `tick.rs` - Periodic Timer Channel

### Core Struct(s) and Fields
- **`Channel`** (lines 61-67)
  - `delivery_time: AtomicCell<Align<Instant>>` - next delivery instant (atomic)
  - `duration: Duration` - fixed interval between ticks
- **`Align<T>`** (lines 19-36): wrapper for 128-bit atomic alignment
- **`TickToken`** (line 17): `type TickToken = Option<Instant>`

### Lazy Message Generation on Receive
- **`try_recv`** (lines 81-98):
  1. Loop with current time and load `delivery_time` (lines 83-84)
  2. Check if `now < delivery_time.0` (line 86)
  3. **Compare-and-swap**: updates `delivery_time` to `now + duration` (lines 90-92)
  4. Returns the old `delivery_time` on successful CAS (line 95)

- **`recv`** (lines 102-130):
  1. Loop loading `delivery_time` (line 104)
  2. Check if deadline expires before delivery_time (line 108)
  3. **Compare-and-swap**: updates to `delivery_time.0.max(now) + duration` (lines 118-120)
  4. Sleep until delivery_time if needed (lines 124-126)
  5. Returns the delivery time that was committed to

### Select-Related Methods (lines 163-209)
- **`try_select`** (lines 165-177): calls `try_recv()`, sets `token.tick = Some(msg)` on success
- **`deadline`** (lines 180-182): always returns `Some(delivery_time.load().0)`
- **`register`** (lines 185-187): returns `self.is_ready()`
- **`accept`** (lines 193-195): calls `try_select(token)`
- **`is_ready`** (lines 198-200): returns `!self.is_empty()`
- **`watch`** (lines 203-205): returns `self.is_ready()`

### Thread Parking/Context/Deadline Interaction
- Uses `thread::sleep(duration)` for blocking (lines 110, 125)
- Does NOT use Context-based thread parking

### Key Concurrency
- **`AtomicCell<Align<Instant>>`** for lock-free atomic access to delivery_time
- **Compare-and-swap loop** ensures exactly one receiver advances the timer
- The CAS operation atomically both: claims the current tick AND schedules the next one
- No Mutex - purely lock-free design

### Approximate Line Numbers
- Align struct: 19-36
- Core Channel struct: 61-67
- try_recv: 81-98
- recv: 102-130
- SelectHandle impl: 163-209

---

## 3. `never.rs` - Empty Channel (Never Delivers)

### Core Struct(s) and Fields
- **`Channel<T>`** (lines 19-21): Zero-sized type
  - `_marker: PhantomData<T>` - only for type parameter variance
- **`NeverToken`** (line 16): `type NeverToken = ()` - doesn't need token storage

### Lazy Message Generation on Receive
- **`try_recv`** (lines 34-36): Always returns `Err(TryRecvError::Empty)`
- **`recv`** (lines 40-43): Calls `utils::sleep_until(deadline)`, then returns `Err(RecvTimeoutError::::Timeout)`

### Select-Related Methods (lines 76-112)
- **`try_select`** (lines 78-80): Always returns `false`
- **`deadline`** (lines 83-85): Always returns `None`
- **`register`** (lines 88-90): Returns `self.is_ready()` (which is `false`)
- **`accept`** (lines 96-98): Calls `try_select(token)` (always returns `false`)
- **`is_ready`** (lines 101-103): Always returns `false`
- **`watch`** (lines 106-108): Returns `self.is_ready()` (always `false`)

### Thread Parking/Context/Deadline Interaction
- Uses `utils::sleep_until(deadline)` which parks the thread until the deadline
- Does NOT use Context-based thread parking for select operations

### Key Concurrency
- **No synchronization primitives** - channel is always empty, no coordination needed
- **Zero-sized type** - compiles away completely
- No atomics, no Mutex

### Approximate Line Numbers
- Core struct: 19-21
- try_recv: 34-36
- recv: 40-43
- SelectHandle impl: 76-112

---

## Summary Comparison

| Aspect | at.rs | tick.rs | never.rs |
|--------|-------|---------|----------|
| **Delivery** | Once at deadline | Periodically | Never |
| **State** | `AtomicBool` + `Instant` | `AtomicCell<Instant>` + `Duration` | Zero-sized |
| **CAS Loop** | No (single swap) | Yes (advances timer) | N/A |
| **Thread Parking** | `thread::sleep` | `thread::sleep` | `utils::sleep_until` |
| **Context Usage** | None | None | None |
| **Select Ready** | `!is_empty()` | `!is_empty()` | Always `false` |</result>

<result># Crossbeam Channel Receiver-Only Flavors Analysis

## 1. `at.rs` - One-Time Timer Channel

### Core Struct(s) and Fields
- **`Channel`** (lines 19-25)
  - `delivery_time: Instant` - the instant when message will be delivered
  - `received: AtomicBool` - tracks whether message has been received
- **`AtToken`** (line 16): `type AtToken = Option<Instant>` - result of receive operation

### Lazy Message Generation on Receive
- **`try_recv`** (lines 39-59):
  1. Optimistic check if already received with `Relaxed` ordering (line 41)
  2. Check if `Instant::now() < delivery_time` (line 46)
  3. Atomically swap `received` flag with `SeqCst` ordering (line 52)
  4. Returns `delivery_time` as the message on success (line 54)

- **`recv`** (lines 63-98):
  1. Optimistic received check (line 65)
  2. Loop comparing current time against `delivery_time` and optional `deadline`
  3. Sleep using `thread::sleep(deadline - now)` (line 86)
  4. Atomically swap `received` flag with `SeqCst` (line 90)
  5. Returns `delivery_time` or blocks forever if already received (line 95)

### Select-Related Methods (lines 143-194)
- **`try_select`** (lines 145-157): calls `try_recv()`, sets `token.at = Some(msg)` on success
- **`deadline`** (lines 160-167): returns `Some(delivery_time)` if not received, `None` otherwise
- **`register`** (lines 170-172): returns `self.is_ready()`
- **`accept`** (lines 178-180): calls `try_select(token)`
- **`is_ready`** (lines 183-185): returns `!self.is_empty()`
- **`watch`** (lines 188-190): returns `self.is_ready()`

### Thread Parking/Context/Deadline Interaction
- Uses `thread::sleep(duration)` for blocking (line 86)
- Uses `utils::sleep_until(deadline)` for indefinite blocking after message consumed (line 95)
- Does NOT use Context-based thread parking

### Key Concurrency
- **`AtomicBool`** for the `received` flag
- **Relaxed ordering** for optimistic checks (lines 41, 65, 110, 162)
- **SeqCst ordering** for the critical swap operation (lines 52, 90, 121)

### Approximate Line Numbers
- Core struct: 19-25
- try_recv: 39-59
- recv: 63-98
- SelectHandle impl: 143-194

---

## 2. `tick.rs` - Periodic Timer Channel

### Core Struct(s) and Fields
- **`Channel`** (lines 61-67)
  - `delivery_time: AtomicCell<Align<Instant>>` - next delivery instant (atomic)
  - `duration: Duration` - fixed interval between ticks
- **`Align<T>`** (lines 19-36): wrapper for 128-bit atomic alignment
- **`TickToken`** (line 17): `type TickToken = Option<Instant>`

### Lazy Message Generation on Receive
- **`try_recv`** (lines 81-98):
  1. Loop with current time and load `delivery_time` (lines 83-84)
  2. Check if `now < delivery_time.0` (line 86)
  3. **Compare-and-swap**: updates `delivery_time` to `now + duration` (lines 90-92)
  4. Returns the old `delivery_time` on successful CAS (line 95)

- **`recv`** (lines 102-130):
  1. Loop loading `delivery_time` (line 104)
  2. Check if deadline expires before delivery_time (line 108)
  3. **Compare-and-swap**: updates to `delivery_time.0.max(now) + duration` (lines 118-120)
  4. Sleep until delivery_time if needed (lines 124-126)
  5. Returns the delivery time that was committed to

### Select-Related Methods (lines 163-209)
- **`try_select`** (lines 165-177): calls `try_recv()`, sets `token.tick = Some(msg)` on success
- **`deadline`** (lines 180-182): always returns `Some(delivery_time.load().0)`
- **`register`** (lines 185-187): returns `self.is_ready()`
- **`accept`** (lines 193-195): calls `try_select(token)`
- **`is_ready`** (lines 198-200): returns `!self.is_empty()`
- **`watch`** (lines 203-205): returns `self.is_ready()`

### Thread Parking/Context/Deadline Interaction
- Uses `thread::sleep(duration)` for blocking (lines 110, 125)
- Does NOT use Context-based thread parking

### Key Concurrency
- **`AtomicCell<Align<Instant>>`** for lock-free atomic access to delivery_time
- **Compare-and-swap loop** ensures exactly one receiver advances the timer
- The CAS operation atomically both: claims the current tick AND schedules the next one
- No Mutex - purely lock-free design

### Approximate Line Numbers
- Align struct: 19-36
- Core Channel struct: 61-67
- try_recv: 81-98
- recv: 102-130
- SelectHandle impl: 163-209

---

## 3. `never.rs` - Empty Channel (Never Delivers)

### Core Struct(s) and Fields
- **`Channel<T>`** (lines 19-21): Zero-sized type
  - `_marker: PhantomData<T>` - only for type parameter variance
- **`NeverToken`** (line 16): `type NeverToken = ()` - doesn't need token storage

### Lazy Message Generation on Receive
- **`try_recv`** (lines 34-36): Always returns `Err(TryRecvError::Empty)`
- **`recv`** (lines 40-43): Calls `utils::sleep_until(deadline)`, then returns `Err(RecvTimeoutError::::Timeout)`

### Select-Related Methods (lines 76-112)
- **`try_select`** (lines 78-80): Always returns `false`
- **`deadline`** (lines 83-85): Always returns `None`
- **`register`** (lines 88-90): Returns `self.is_ready()` (which is `false`)
- **`accept`** (lines 96-98): Calls `try_select(token)` (always returns `false`)
- **`is_ready`** (lines 101-103): Always returns `false`
- **`watch`** (lines 106-108): Returns `self.is_ready()` (always `false`)

### Thread Parking/Context/Deadline Interaction
- Uses `utils::sleep_until(deadline)` which parks the thread until the deadline
- Does NOT use Context-based thread parking for select operations

### Key Concurrency
- **No synchronization primitives** - channel is always empty, no coordination needed
- **Zero-sized type** - compiles away completely
- No atomics, no Mutex

### Approximate Line Numbers
- Core struct: 19-21
- try_recv: 34-36
- recv: 40-43
- SelectHandle impl: 76-112

---

## Summary Comparison

| Aspect | at.rs | tick.rs | never.rs |
|--------|-------|---------|----------|
| **Delivery** | Once at deadline | Periodically | Never |
| **State** | `AtomicBool` + `Instant` | `AtomicCell<Instant>` + `Duration` | Zero-sized |
| **CAS Loop** | No (single swap) | Yes (advances timer) | N/A |
| **Thread Parking** | `thread::sleep` | `thread::sleep` | `utils::sleep_until` |
| **Context Usage** | None | None | None |
| **Select Ready** | `!is_empty()` | `!is_empty()` | Always `false` |</result>

<result># Crossbeam Channel Flavors Analysis

## 1. Array Flavor (array.rs) - Bounded Channel

### Core Structs and Fields

**Slot<T>** (lines 30-37):
- `stamp: AtomicUsize` - Version stamp for synchronization
- `msg: UnsafeCell<MaybeUninit<T>>` - Message storage

**ArrayToken** (lines 40-47):
- `slot: *const u8` - Pointer to the reserved slot
- `stamp: usize` - Stamp value to write after operation

**Channel<T>** (lines 60-93):
- `head: CachePadded<AtomicUsize>` - Head position stamp (index + lap, no mark bit)
- `tail: CachePadded<AtomicUsize>` - Tail position stamp (index + lap + mark bit for disconnect)
- `buffer: Box<[Slot<T>]>` - Preallocated ring buffer
- `one_lap: usize` - Constant = `mark_bit * 2`
- `mark_bit: usize` - Bit indicating disconnect in tail = `(cap + 1).next_power_of_two()`
- `senders: SyncWaker` - Waiting senders queue
- `receivers: SyncWaker` - Waiting receivers queue

**Stamp encoding**: `{ lap, mark, index }` packed in single usize. Mark bit only in tail.

### Send Operations

**try_send** (lines 323-331):
1. Calls `start_send` to reserve slot
2. Calls `unsafe write` to store message
3. Returns `Ok(())` or `Err(TrySendError::Full(msg))` / `Err(TrySendError::Disconnected(msg))`

**send with deadline** (lines 333-384):
1. Loop: try `start_send` with backoff
2. If ready, call `write` and return
3. If full after backoff: check deadline, enter `Context::with` block
4. Register with `senders.register(oper, cx)`, call `cx.wait_until(deadline)`
5. On wake, retry or return timeout/disconnected

**start_send** (lines 143-212):
- CAS loop on `tail` to reserve slot
- Checks disconnect via `tail & mark_bit`
- Deconstructs tail into index/lap, inspects slot stamp
- Returns `true` with token populated (or null for disconnect)

**write** (lines 214-230):
- Check if slot null (disconnected)
- Write message with `UnsafeCell::write`
- Store new stamp with `Ordering::Release`
- Call `receivers.notify()`

### Receive Operations

**try_recv** (lines 387-395): Similar to try_send, calls `start_recv` then `read`

**recv with deadline** (lines 397-446): Mirrors send flow, uses `receivers.register`

**start_recv** (lines 233-303):
- CAS loop on `head` to reserve slot
- Checks empty: `(tail & !mark_bit) == head`
- Checks disconnect: `tail & mark_bit != 0`

**read** (lines 305-321):
- Read message with `UnsafeCell::read().assume_init()`
- Store new stamp (head + one_lap) with `Ordering::Release`
- Call `senders.notify()`

### SelectHandle Implementation (Receiver/Sender)

**try_select** (lines 614-616, 650-652):
- Receiver: calls `start_recv(token)`
- Sender: calls `start_send(token)`

**register** (lines 622-625, 658-661):
- Calls `senders.register` or `receivers.register(oper, cx)`
- Returns `is_ready()`

**accept** (lines 631-633, 667-669):
- Calls `try_select(token)` again to re-verify

**is_ready** (lines 635-637, 671-673):
- Receiver: `!is_empty() || is_disconnected()`
- Sender: `!is_full() || is_disconnected()`

**watch/unwatch** (lines 639-646, 675-682): Delegates to SyncWaker

### Disconnect and Wake

**disconnect_senders** (lines 487-496):
- `tail.fetch_or(mark_bit, SeqCst)` sets disconnect bit
- Calls `receivers.disconnect()` to wake all waiting receivers

**disconnect_receivers** (lines 506-517):
- Sets disconnect bit
- Calls `senders.disconnect()`
- Calls `discard_all_messages` to drop remaining messages

**discard_all_messages** (lines 531-575): Iterates from head to tail, dropping messages with backoff waits for concurrent writes

### Concurrency Techniques

- **Lock-free**: No mutex, pure atomic operations
- **CAS loops**: `compare_exchange_weak(SeqCst, Relaxed)` on head/tail
- **Memory ordering**: Acquire for loads, Release for stamp stores, SeqCst for index CAS
- **Backoff**: `Backoff::new()` with `spin()` and `snooze()` for contention
- **CachePadded**: Prevents false sharing on head/tail
- **Unsafe**: Direct pointer manipulation, MaybeUninit for uninitialized memory

---

## 2. List Flavor (list.rs) - Unbounded Channel

### Core Structs and Fields

**Constants** (lines 30-47):
- `WRITE = 1`, `READ = 2`, `DESTROY = 4` - Slot state bits
- `LAP = 32`, `BLOCK_CAP = 31`
- `SHIFT = 1` - Lower bits reserved for metadata
- `MARK_BIT = 1` - In head: not last block; in tail: disconnected

**Slot<T>** (lines 50-56):
- `msg: UnsafeCell<MaybeUninit<T>>`
- `state: AtomicUsize` - State bits (WRITE/READ/DESTROY)

**Block<T>** (lines 68-77):
- `next: AtomicPtr<Block<T>>` - Next block in list
- `slots: [Slot<T>; BLOCK_CAP]` - Fixed 31 slots

**Position<T>** (lines 141-148):
- `index: AtomicUsize` - Message index in channel
- `block: AtomicPtr<Block<T>>` - Current block pointer

**ListToken** (lines 152-158):
- `block: *const u8` - Reserved block
- `offset: usize` - Slot offset within block

**Channel<T>** (lines 171-189):
- `head: CachePadded<Position<T>>` - Read position
- `tail: CachePadded<Position<T>>` - Write position
- `receivers: SyncWaker` - Waiting receivers only (senders never block)
- `_marker: PhantomData<T>`

**Index encoding**: `{ block_index, unused_bit }` - `(index >> SHIFT) % LAP` gives offset

### Send Operations

**try_send** (lines 433-438): Calls `send(msg, None)`

**send with deadline** (lines 441-452): 
- Ignores deadline (unbounded, never full)
- Calls `start_send` then `unsafe write`

**start_send** (lines 219-299):
1. Load tail index and block with `Ordering::Acquire`
2. Check disconnect: `tail & MARK_BIT`
3. Calculate offset: `(tail >> SHIFT) % LAP`
4. If offset == BLOCK_CAP, wait for next block installation
5. If first message (block null), allocate and install first block with CAS
6. Pre-allocate next block if `offset + 1 == BLOCK_CAP`
7. CAS on tail.index: `tail -> tail + (1 << SHIFT)`
8. If at block end, install next block and link it

**write** (lines 302-318):
- Check block null (disconnected)
- Write message with `UnsafeCell::write`
- `slot.state.fetch_or(WRITE, Release)`
- `receivers.notify()`

### Receive Operations

**try_recv** (lines 455-463): Calls `start_recv` then `read`

**recv with deadline** (lines 466-515): 
- Loop with backoff calling `start_recv`/`read`
- If empty after backoff, register with `receivers`, wait via `cx.wait_until`

**start_recv** (lines 321-403):
1. Load head index and block
2. Calculate offset, wait if at BLOCK_CAP
3. Check empty: `(head >> SHIFT) == (tail >> SHIFT)`
4. Check disconnect in tail
5. If head/tail in different blocks, set MARK_BIT in new_head
6. CAS on head.index
7. If at block end, wait for next, move to it

**read** (lines 406-430):
- `slot.wait_write()` - spin until WRITE bit set
- Read message
- If at block end or DESTROY bit set, call `Block::destroy`

**Block::destroy** (lines 120-137):
- Iterate slots from start, setting DESTROY bit
- If any slot still in use (no READ bit), return early
- Otherwise deallocate block with `Box::from_raw`

### SelectHandle Implementation

**Receiver** (lines 716-749):
- `try_select`: calls `start_recv`
- `register`: delegates to `receivers.register`, returns `is_ready()`
- `accept`: calls `try_select`
- `is_ready`: `!is_empty() || is_disconnected()`
- `watch/unwatch`: delegates to SyncWaker

**Sender** (lines 752-780):
- `try_select`: calls `start_send`
- `register/unwatch`: no-ops (sender always ready)
- `accept`: calls `try_select`
- `is_ready`: always `true` (unbounded)
- `watch`: no-op

### Disconnect and Wake

**disconnect_senders** (lines 561-569):
- `tail.index.fetch_or(MARK_BIT, SeqCst)`
- `receivers.disconnect()`

**disconnect_receivers** (lines 575-586):
- Sets MARK_BIT
- Calls `discard_all_messages` to free memory

**discard_all_messages** (lines 591-653):
- Swaps head.block to null (prevents new sends)
- Iterates from head to tail, dropping messages
- Deallocates all blocks

### Concurrency Techniques

- **Lock-free**: CAS on indices only
- **Memory ordering**: Acquire for loads, Release for stores, SeqCst for index CAS
- **Lazy block deallocation**: DESTROY bit handoff to reader
- **Backoff**: Wait loops for next block pointer
- **Unsafe**: Raw pointer manipulation, MaybeUninit, Box::from_raw
- **Allocation**: Global allocator with zeroed Box allocation

---

## 3. Zero Flavor (zero.rs) - Rendezvous Channel

### Core Structs and Fields

**ZeroToken** (lines 25-26):
- `*mut ()` - Raw pointer to Packet

**Packet<T>** (lines 41-50):
- `on_stack: bool` - Allocation location
- `ready: AtomicBool` - Synchronization flag
- `msg: UnsafeCell<Option<T>>` - Message storage

**Inner** (lines 90-99):
- `senders: Waker` - Waiting sender operations
- `receivers: Waker` - Waiting receiver operations
- `is_disconnected: bool` - Channel state

**Channel<T>** (lines 102-108):
- `inner: Mutex<Inner>` - Protected state
- `_marker: PhantomData<T>`

### Send Operations

**try_send** (lines 205-222):
1. Lock inner mutex
2. Try `receivers.try_select()` - if waiting receiver exists, pair up
3. If paired, write message to packet, return Ok
4. If disconnected, return Disconnected error
5. Otherwise return Full error (no receiver waiting)

**send with deadline** (lines 225-279):
1. Lock mutex
2. If receiver waiting, pair up immediately
3. If disconnected, return error
4. Otherwise, enter `Context::with`:
   - Create `Packet::message_on_stack(msg)` on stack
   - `senders.register_with_packet(oper, &mut packet, cx)`
   - `receivers.notify()` to wake potential receiver
   - Drop lock, call `cx.wait_until(deadline)`
5. On wake: if Aborted/Disconnected, unregister and return error with message
6. If Operation selected, `packet.wait_ready()` for receiver to read

**start_send** (lines 134-147): Locks, tries `receivers.try_select()`, sets token

**write** (lines 150-160):
- Check packet null
- Write message into `packet.msg`
- `packet.ready.store(true, Release)`

### Receive Operations

**try_recv** (lines 282-296): Mirrors try_send, uses `senders.try_select()`

**recv with deadline** (lines 299-348):
1. Lock mutex, try `senders.try_select()`
2. If sender waiting, read and return immediately
3. Otherwise, enter `Context::with`:
   - Create `Packet::empty_on_stack()` 
   - `receivers.register_with_packet(oper, &mut packet, cx)`
   - `senders.notify()`
   - Drop lock, wait via `cx.wait_until(deadline)`
4. On wake: check selected status
5. If Operation, `packet.wait_ready()` for sender to write, extract message

**start_recv** (lines 163-176): Locks, tries `senders.try_select()`

**read** (lines 179-202):
- Check packet null (disconnected)
- If `packet.on_stack`: message already present, read and set ready
- Otherwise: `packet.wait_ready()` for write, read message, deallocate packet

**Packet::wait_ready** (lines 81-86):
- Spin-wait with backoff until `ready.load(Acquire)` is true

### SelectHandle Implementation

**Receiver** (lines 393-441):
- `try_select`: calls `start_recv`
- `register`: allocates heap packet with `Packet::empty_on_heap()`, locks, calls `receivers.register_with_packet`, notifies senders, returns ready state
- `unregister`: deallocates heap packet if still present
- `accept`: `token.zero.0 = cx.wait_packet()` - retrieves packet from context
- `is_ready`: locks, checks `senders.can_select() || is_disconnected`
- `watch`: locks, calls `receivers.watch`, checks ready

**Sender** (lines 443-491): Symmetric to receiver, uses `senders` waker

### Disconnect and Wake

**disconnect** (lines 353-364):
- Lock mutex
- Set `is_disconnected = true`
- `senders.disconnect()` and `receivers.disconnect()`

### Concurrency Techniques

- **Mutex protected**: All channel state protected by `Mutex<Inner>`
- **Rendezvous pairing**: Waker coordinates sender-receiver matching
- **Stack allocation**: Packets allocated on stack in non-select paths
- **Heap allocation**: Packets allocated on heap for select operations
- **Spin-wait**: `wait_ready` with backoff for ready flag
- **Memory ordering**: Acquire for ready loads, Release for stores
- **Unsafe**: Raw packet pointer manipulation, Box::from_raw for cleanup</result>

<result># Crossbeam-Channel Select Mechanism Report

## 1. Key Types

### `Token` (lines 18-32)
Temporary data initialized during select/blocking operations, consumed by `read` or `write`. Contains flavor-specific tokens:
- `at: flavors::at::AtToken`
- `array: flavors::array::ArrayToken`
- `list: flavors::list::ListToken`
- `never: flavors::never::NeverToken`
- `tick: flavors::tick::TickToken`
- `zero: flavors::zero::ZeroToken`

### `Operation` (lines 35-52)
Identifier for an operation by a specific thread on a specific channel. Created via `Operation::hook<T>(r: &mut T) -> Self` which converts a mutable reference's address to a usize (must be > 2 to avoid conflict with Selected enum values).

### `Selected` (lines 56-68)
Enum representing operation state:
- `Waiting` - still waiting for an operation (value 0)
- `Aborted` - blocking attempt aborted (value 1)
- `Disconnected` - operation ready due to disconnection (value 2)
- `Operation(Operation)` - operation ready for send/receive (value > 2)

Implements `From<usize>` and `From<Selected>` for usize conversions.

### `SelectHandle` trait (lines 99-157)
Methods for channel endpoints participating in select:
- `try_select(&self, token: &mut Token) -> bool` - attempt selection without blocking
- `deadline(&self) -> Option<Instant>` - returns deadline if any
- `register(&self, oper: Operation, cx: &Context) -> bool` - register for execution, returns true if now ready
- `unregister(&self, oper: Operation)` - unregister operation
- `accept(&self, token: &mut Token, cx: &Context) -> bool` - accept operation thread was woken for
- `is_ready(&self) -> bool` - check if operation can execute without blocking
- `watch(&self, oper: Operation, cx: &Context) -> bool` - register for readiness notification
- `unwatch(&self, oper: Operation)` - unregister readiness notification

Also implemented for `&T` where `T: SelectHandle`.

### `Select<'a>` struct (lines 616-625)
```rust
pub struct Select<'a> {
    handles: Vec<(&'a dyn SelectHandle, usize, usize)>,  // (handle, index, address)
    next_index: usize,                                     // next index to assign
    biased: bool,                                          // biased vs unbiased selection
}
```

### `SelectedOperation<'a>` (lines 1209-1331)
Must-complete wrapper for selected operations:
```rust
pub struct SelectedOperation<'a> {
    token: Token,              // for completion
    index: usize,              // selected operation index
    addr: usize,               // address of Sender/Receiver
    _marker: PhantomData<&'a ()>,
}
```
Methods: `index()`, `send<T>()`, `recv<T>()`. **Panics on Drop if not completed.**

### Helper Functions
- `receiver_addr<T>(s: &Receiver<T>) -> usize` (lines 524-526)
- `sender_addr<T>(s: &Sender<T>) -> usize` (lines 523-525)

## 2. Overall Selection Algorithm

### `Timeout` enum (lines 161-170)
Controls blocking behavior:
- `Now` - no blocking
- `Never` - block forever
- `At(Instant)` - block until deadline

### `run_select` function (lines 176-324)

**Steps:**

1. **Empty handle check** (lines 181-194): If empty, sleep until timeout and return None

2. **Shuffle for fairness** (lines 196-199): If not biased, randomize handle order via `utils::shuffle`

3. **Fast path try_select loop** (lines 207-211): Try each handle with `try_select`; return immediately if successful

4. **Registration phase** (lines 213-246):
   - Create `Context` with `Context::with`
   - For each handle: call `register(Operation::hook(handle), cx)`
   - If registration returns `true`, operation became ready during registration
   - Abort registration via `cx.try_select(Selected::Aborted)`
   - Stop if another thread selected an operation

5. **Deadline computation** (lines 248-260): Calculate minimum deadline from all handles

6. **Blocking wait** (line 263): `sel = cx.wait_until(deadline)` - parks thread

7. **Unregistration** (lines 267-269): Unregister all registered operations

8. **Result handling** (lines 271-297):
   - `Aborted`: Try selecting ready operation from registration
   - `Disconnected`: Return None
   - `Operation(op)`: Find matching handle and call `accept`

9. **Post-wakeup retry** (lines 303-312): After wake, retry try_select loop

10. **Timeout check** (lines 314-322): Return None if timeout expired

### `run_ready` function (lines 327-451)
Similar structure but uses `is_ready`/`watch`/`unwatch` instead of `try_select`/`register`/`unregister`/`accept`. Returns only the index, not a Token.

## 3. `Select<'a>` Public API

### Construction (lines 643-670)
- `new()` - Creates unbiased selector
- `new_biased()` - Creates biased selector (lowest index wins when multiple ready)

### Adding Operations (lines 686-714)
- `send<T>(&mut self, s: &'a Sender<T>) -> usize` - Add send op, return index
- `recv<T>(&mut self, r: &'a Receiver<T>) -> usize` - Add recv op, return index

### Management (lines 752-769)
- `remove(&mut self, index: usize)` - Remove operation by index

### Selection Methods (return SelectedOperation)

**Non-blocking:**
- `try_select(&mut self) -> Result<SelectedOperation<'a>, TrySelectError>` (lines 809-811)

**Blocking:**
- `select(&mut self) -> SelectedOperation<'a>` (lines 860-862) - panics if empty
- `select_timeout(&mut self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<SelectedOperation<'a>, SelectTimeoutError>` (lines 911-916)
- `select_deadline(&mut self, deadline: Instant) -> Result<SelectedOperation<'a>, SelectTimeoutError>` (lines 967-972)

### Readiness Methods (return index only)

**Non-blocking:**
- `try_ready(&mut self) -> Result<usize, TryReadyError>` (lines 1009-1014)

**Blocking:**
- `ready(&mut self) -> usize` (lines 1062-1068) - panics if empty
- `ready_timeout(&mut self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<usize, ReadyTimeoutError>` (lines 1114-1119)
- `ready_deadline(&mut self, deadline: Instant) -> Result<usize, ReadyTimeoutError>` (lines 1167-1172)

### `SelectedOperation` Completion (lines 1224-1319)
- `index(&self) -> usize` - Get selected index

# Crossbeam-Channel Select Mechanism Report

## 1. Key Types

### `Token` (lines 18-32)
Temporary data initialized during select/blocking operations, consumed by `read` or `write`. Contains flavor-specific tokens:
- `at: flavors::at::AtToken`
- `array: flavors::array::ArrayToken`
- `list: flavors::list::ListToken`
- `never: flavors::never::NeverToken`
- `tick: flavors::tick::TickToken`
- `zero: flavors::zero::ZeroToken`

### `Operation` (lines 35-52)
Identifier for an operation by a specific thread on a specific channel. Created via `Operation::hook<T>(r: &mut T) -> Self` which converts a mutable reference's address to a usize (must be > 2 to avoid conflict with Selected enum values).

### `Selected` (lines 56-68)
Enum representing operation state:
- `Waiting` - still waiting for an operation (value 0)
- `Aborted` - blocking attempt aborted (value 1)
- `Disconnected` - operation ready due to disconnection (value 2)
- `Operation(Operation)` - operation ready for send/receive (value > 2)

Implements `From<usize>` and `From<Selected>` for usize conversions.

### `SelectHandle` trait (lines 99-157)
Methods for channel endpoints participating in select:
- `try_select(&self, token: &mut Token) -> bool` - attempt selection without blocking
- `deadline(&self) -> Option<Instant>` - returns deadline if any
- `register(&self, oper: Operation, cx: &Context) -> bool` - register for execution, returns true if now ready
- `unregister(&self, oper: Operation)` - unregister operation
- `accept(&self, token: &mut Token, cx: &Context) -> bool` - accept operation thread was woken for
- `is_ready(&self) -> bool` - check if operation can execute without blocking
- `watch(&self, oper: Operation, cx: &Context) -> bool` - register for readiness notification
- `unwatch(&self, oper: Operation)` - unregister readiness notification

Also implemented for `&T` where `T: SelectHandle`.

### `Select<'a>` struct (lines 616-625)
```rust
pub struct Select<'a> {
    handles: Vec<(&'a dyn SelectHandle, usize, usize)>,  // (handle, index, address)
    next_index: usize,                                     // next index to assign
    biased: bool,                                          // biased vs unbiased selection
}
```

### `SelectedOperation<'a>` (lines 1209-1331)
Must-complete wrapper for selected operations:
```rust

    token: Token,              // for completion
    index: usize,              // selected operation index
    addr: usize,               // address of Sender/Receiver
    _marker: PhantomData<&'a ()>,
}
```
Methods: `index()`, `send<T>()`, `recv<T>()`. **Panics on Drop if not completed.**

### Helper Functions
- `receiver_addr<T>(s: &Receiver<T>) -> usize` (lines 524-526)
- `sender_addr<T>(s: &Sender<T>) -> usize` (lines 523-525)

## 2. Overall Selection Algorithm

### `Timeout` enum (lines 161-170)
Controls blocking behavior:
- `Now` - no blocking
- `Never` - block forever
- `At(Instant)` - block until deadline

### `run_select` function (lines 176-324)

**Steps:**

1. **Empty handle check** (lines 181-194): If empty, sleep until timeout and return None

2. **Shuffle for fairness** (lines 196-199): If not biased, randomize handle order via `utils::shuffle`

3. **Fast path try_select loop** (lines 207-211): Try each handle with `try_select`; return immediately if successful

4. **Registration phase** (lines 213-246):
   - Create `Context` with `Context::with`
   - For each handle: call `register(Operation::hook(handle), cx)`
   - If registration returns `true`, operation became ready during registration
   - Abort registration via `cx.try_select(Selected::Aborted)`
   - Stop if another thread selected an operation

5. **Deadline computation** (lines 248-260): Calculate minimum deadline from all handles

6. **Blocking wait** (line 263): `sel = cx.wait_until(deadline)` - parks thread

7. **Unregistration** (lines 267-269): Unregister all registered operations

8. **Result handling** (lines 271-297):
   - `Aborted`: Try selecting ready operation from registration
   - `Disconnected`: Return None
   - `Operation(op)`: Find matching handle and call `accept`

9. **Post-wakeup retry** (lines 303-312): After wake, retry try_select loop

10. **Timeout check** (lines 314-322): Return None if timeout expired

### `run_ready` function (lines 327-451)
Similar structure but uses `is_ready`/`watch`/`unwatch` instead of `try_select`/`register`/`unregister`/`accept`. Returns only the index, not a Token.

- `new()` - Creates unbiased selector

<result># Crossbeam Channel Flavors Analysis

## 1. Array Flavor (array.rs) - Bounded Channel

### Core Structs and Fields

**Slot<T>** (lines 30-37):
- `stamp: AtomicUsize` - Version stamp for synchronization
- `msg: UnsafeCell<MaybeUninit<T>>` - Message storage

**ArrayToken** (lines 40-47):
- `slot: *const u8` - Pointer to the reserved slot
- `stamp: usize` - Stamp value to write after operation

**Channel<T>** (lines 60-93):
- `head: CachePadded<AtomicUsize>` - Head position stamp (index + lap, no mark bit)
- `tail: CachePadded<AtomicUsize>` - Tail position stamp (index + lap + mark bit for disconnect)
- `buffer: Box<[Slot<T>]>` - Preallocated ring buffer
- `one_lap: usize` - Constant = `mark_bit * 2`
- `mark_bit: usize` - Bit indicating disconnect in tail = `(cap + 1).next_power_of_two()`
- `senders: SyncWaker` - Waiting senders queue
- `receivers: SyncWaker` - Waiting receivers queue

**Stamp encoding**: `{ lap, mark, index }` packed in single usize. Mark bit only in tail.

### Send Operations

**try_send** (lines 323-331):
1. Calls `start_send` to reserve slot
2. Calls `unsafe write` to store message
3. Returns `Ok(())` or `Err(TrySendError::Full(msg))` / `Err(TrySendError::Disconnected(msg))`

**send with deadline** (lines 333-384):
1. Loop: try `start_send` with backoff
2. If ready, call `write` and return
3. If full after backoff: check deadline, enter `Context::with` block
4. Register with `senders.register(oper, cx)`, call `cx.wait_until(deadline)`
5. On wake, retry or return timeout/disconnected

**start_send** (lines 143-212):
- CAS loop on `tail` to reserve slot
- Checks disconnect via `tail & mark_bit`
- Deconstructs tail into index/lap, inspects slot stamp
- Returns `true` with token populated (or null for disconnect)

**write** (lines 214-230):
- Check if slot null (disconnected)
- Write message with `UnsafeCell::write`
- Store new stamp with `Ordering::Release`
- Call `receivers.notify()`

### Receive Operations

**try_recv** (lines 387-395): Similar to try_send, calls `start_recv` then `read`

**recv with deadline** (lines 397-446): Mirrors send flow, uses `receivers.register`

**start_recv** (lines 233-303):
- CAS loop on `head` to reserve slot
- Checks empty: `(tail & !mark_bit) == head`
- Checks disconnect: `tail & mark_bit != 0`

**read** (lines 305-321):
- Read message with `UnsafeCell::read().assume_init()`
- Store new stamp (head + one_lap) with `Ordering::Release`
- Call `senders.notify()`

### SelectHandle Implementation (Receiver/Sender)

**try_select** (lines 614-616, 650-652):
- Receiver: calls `start_recv(token)`
- Sender: calls `start_send(token)`

**register** (lines 622-625, 658-661):
- Calls `senders.register` or `receivers.register(oper, cx)`
- Returns `is_ready()`

**accept** (lines 631-633, 667-669):
- Calls `try_select(token)` again to re-verify

**is_ready** (lines 635-637, 671-673):
- Receiver: `!is_empty() || is_disconnected()`
- Sender: `!is_full() || is_disconnected()`

**watch/unwatch** (lines 639-646, 675-682): Delegates to SyncWaker

### Disconnect and Wake

**disconnect_senders** (lines 487-496):
- `tail.fetch_or(mark_bit, SeqCst)` sets disconnect bit
- Calls `receivers.disconnect()` to wake all waiting receivers

**disconnect_receivers** (lines 506-517):
- Sets disconnect bit
- Calls `senders.disconnect()`
- Calls `discard_all_messages` to drop remaining messages

**discard_all_messages** (lines 531-575): Iterates from head to tail, dropping messages with backoff waits for concurrent writes

### Concurrency Techniques

- **Lock-free**: No mutex, pure atomic operations
- **CAS loops**: `compare_exchange_weak(SeqCst, Relaxed)` on head/tail
- **Memory ordering**: Acquire for loads, Release for stamp stores, SeqCst for index CAS
- **Backoff**: `Backoff::new()` with `spin()` and `snooze()` for contention
- **CachePadded**: Prevents false sharing on head/tail
- **Unsafe**: Direct pointer manipulation, MaybeUninit for uninitialized memory

---

## 2. List Flavor (list.rs) - Unbounded Channel

### Core Structs and Fields

**Constants** (lines 30-47):
- `WRITE = 1`, `READ = 2`, `DESTROY = 4` - Slot state bits
- `LAP = 32`, `BLOCK_CAP = 31`
- `SHIFT = 1` - Lower bits reserved for metadata
- `MARK_BIT = 1` - In head: not last block; in tail: disconnected

**Slot<T>** (lines 50-56):
- `msg: UnsafeCell<MaybeUninit<T>>`
- `state: AtomicUsize` - State bits (WRITE/READ/DESTROY)

**Block<T>** (lines 68-77):
- `next: AtomicPtr<Block<T>>` - Next block in list
- `slots: [Slot<T>; BLOCK_CAP]` - Fixed 31 slots

**Position<T>** (lines 141-148):
- `index: AtomicUsize` - Message index in channel
- `block: AtomicPtr<Block<T>>` - Current block pointer

**ListToken** (lines 152-158):
- `block: *const u8` - Reserved block
- `offset: usize` - Slot offset within block

**Channel<T>** (lines 171-189):
- `head: CachePadded<Position<T>>` - Read position
- `tail: CachePadded<Position<T>>` - Write position
- `receivers: SyncWaker` - Waiting receivers only (senders never block)
- `_marker: PhantomData<T>`

**Index encoding**: `{ block_index, unused_bit }` - `(index >> SHIFT) % LAP` gives offset

### Send Operations

**try_send** (lines 433-438): Calls `send(msg, None)`

**send with deadline** (lines 441-452): 
- Ignores deadline (unbounded, never full)
- Calls `start_send` then `unsafe write`

**start_send** (lines 219-299):
1. Load tail index and block with `Ordering::Acquire`
2. Check disconnect: `tail & MARK_BIT`
3. Calculate offset: `(tail >> SHIFT) % LAP`
4. If offset == BLOCK_CAP, wait for next block installation
5. If first message (block null), allocate and install first block with CAS
6. Pre-allocate next block if `offset + 1 == BLOCK_CAP`
7. CAS on tail.index: `tail -> tail + (1 << SHIFT)`
8. If at block end, install next block and link it

**write** (lines 302-318):
- Check block null (disconnected)
- Write message with `UnsafeCell::write`
- `slot.state.fetch_or(WRITE, Release)`
- `receivers.notify()`

### Receive Operations

**try_recv** (lines 455-463): Calls `start_recv` then `read`

**recv with deadline** (lines 466-515): 
- Loop with backoff calling `start_recv`/`read`
- If empty after backoff, register with `receivers`, wait via `cx.wait_until`

**start_recv** (lines 321-403):
1. Load head index and block
2. Calculate offset, wait if at BLOCK_CAP
3. Check empty: `(head >> SHIFT) == (tail >> SHIFT)`
4. Check disconnect in tail
5. If head/tail in different blocks, set MARK_BIT in new_head
6. CAS on head.index
7. If at block end, wait for next, move to it

**read** (lines 406-430):
- `slot.wait_write()` - spin until WRITE bit set
- Read message
- If at block end or DESTROY bit set, call `Block::destroy`

**Block::destroy** (lines 120-137):
- Iterate slots from start, setting DESTROY bit
- If any slot still in use (no READ bit), return early
- Otherwise deallocate block with `Box::from_raw`

### SelectHandle Implementation

**Receiver** (lines 716-749):
- `try_select`: calls `start_recv`
- `register`: delegates to `receivers.register`, returns `is_ready()`
- `accept`: calls `try_select`
- `is_ready`: `!is_empty() || is_disconnected()`
- `watch/unwatch`: delegates to SyncWaker

**Sender** (lines 752-780):
- `try_select`: calls `start_send`

<result># Crossbeam-Channel Select Mechanism Report

## 1. Key Types

### `Token` (lines 18-32)
Temporary data initialized during select/blocking operations, consumed by `read` or `write`. Contains flavor-specific tokens:
- `at: flavors::at::AtToken`
- `array: flavors::array::ArrayToken`
- `list: flavors::list::ListToken`
- `never: flavors::never::NeverToken`
- `tick: flavors::tick::TickToken`
- `zero: flavors::zero::ZeroToken`

### `Operation` (lines 35-52)
Identifier for an operation by a specific thread on a specific channel. Created via `Operation::hook<T>(r: &mut T) -> Self` which converts a mutable reference's address to a usize (must be > 2 to avoid conflict with Selected enum values).

### `Selected` (lines 56-68)
Enum representing operation state:
- `Waiting` - still waiting for an operation (value 0)
- `Aborted` - blocking attempt aborted (value 1)
- `Disconnected` - operation ready due to disconnection (value 2)
- `Operation(Operation)` - operation ready for send/receive (value > 2)

Implements `From<usize>` and `From<Selected>` for usize conversions.

### `SelectHandle` trait (lines 99-157)
Methods for channel endpoints participating in select:
- `try_select(&self, token: &mut Token) -> bool` - attempt selection without blocking
- `deadline(&self) -> Option<Instant>` - returns deadline if any
- `register(&self, oper: Operation, cx: &Context) -> bool` - register for execution, returns true if now ready
- `unregister(&self, oper: Operation)` - unregister operation
- `accept(&self, token: &mut Token, cx: &Context) -> bool` - accept operation thread was woken for
- `is_ready(&self) -> bool` - check if operation can execute without blocking
- `watch(&self, oper: Operation, cx: &Context) -> bool` - register for readiness notification
- `unwatch(&self, oper: Operation)` - unregister readiness notification

Also implemented for `&T` where `T: SelectHandle`.

### `Select<'a>` struct (lines 616-625)
```rust
pub struct Select<'a> {
    handles: Vec<(&'a dyn SelectHandle, usize, usize)>,  // (handle, index, address)
    next_index: usize,                                     // next index to assign
    biased: bool,                                          // biased vs unbiased selection
}
```

### `SelectedOperation<'a>` (lines 1209-1331)
Must-complete wrapper for selected operations:
```rust
pub struct SelectedOperation<'a> {
    token: Token,              // for completion
    index: usize,              // selected operation index
    addr: usize,               // address of Sender/Receiver
    _marker: PhantomData<&'a ()>,
}
```
Methods: `index()`, `send<T>()`, `recv<T>()`. **Panics on Drop if not completed.**

### Helper Functions
- `receiver_addr<T>(s: &Receiver<T>) -> usize` (lines 524-526)
- `sender_addr<T>(s: &Sender<T>) -> usize` (lines 523-525)

## 2. Overall Selection Algorithm

### `Timeout` enum (lines 161-170)
Controls blocking behavior:
- `Now` - no blocking
- `Never` - block forever
- `At(Instant)` - block until deadline

### `run_select` function (lines 176-324)

**Steps:**

1. **Empty handle check** (lines 181-194): If empty, sleep until timeout and return None

2. **Shuffle for fairness** (lines 196-199): If not biased, randomize handle order via `utils::shuffle`

3. **Fast path try_select loop** (lines 207-211): Try each handle with `try_select`; return immediately if successful

4. **Registration phase** (lines 213-246):
   - Create `Context` with `Context::with`
   - For each handle: call `register(Operation::hook(handle), cx)`
   - If registration returns `true`, operation became ready during registration
   - Abort registration via `cx.try_select(Selected::Aborted)`
   - Stop if another thread selected an operation

5. **Deadline computation** (lines 248-260): Calculate minimum deadline from all handles

6. **Blocking wait** (line 263): `sel = cx.wait_until(deadline)` - parks thread

7. **Unregistration** (lines 267-269): Unregister all registered operations

8. **Result handling** (lines 271-297):
   - `Aborted`: Try selecting ready operation from registration
   - `Disconnected`: Return None
   - `Operation(op)`: Find matching handle and call `accept`

9. **Post-wakeup retry** (lines 303-312): After wake, retry try_select loop

10. **Timeout check** (lines 314-322): Return None if timeout expired

### `run_ready` function (lines 327-451)
Similar structure but uses `is_ready`/`watch`/`unwatch` instead of `try_select`/`register`/`unregister`/`accept`. Returns only the index, not a Token.

## 3. `Select<'a>` Public API

### Construction (lines 643-670)
- `new()` - Creates unbiased selector
- `new_biased()` - Creates biased selector (lowest index wins when multiple ready)

### Adding Operations (lines 686-714)
- `send<T>(&mut self, s: &'a Sender<T>) -> usize` - Add send op, return index
- `recv<T>(&mut self, r: &'a Receiver<T>) -> usize` - Add recv op, return index

### Management (lines 752-769)
- `remove(&mut self, index: usize)` - Remove operation by index

### Selection Methods (return SelectedOperation)

**Non-blocking:**
- `try_select(&mut self) -> Result<SelectedOperation<'a>, TrySelectError>` (lines 809-811)

**Blocking:**
- `select(&mut self) -> SelectedOperation<'a>` (lines 860-862) - panics if empty
- `select_timeout(&mut self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<SelectedOperation<'a>, SelectTimeoutError>` (lines 911-916)
- `select_deadline(&mut self, deadline: Instant) -> Result<SelectedOperation<'a>, SelectTimeoutError>` (lines 967-972)

**Non-blocking:**
- `try_ready(&mut self) -> Result<usize, TryReadyError>` (lines 1009-1014)

**Blocking:**
- `ready(&mut self) -> usize` (lines 1062-1068) - panics if empty
- `ready_timeout(&mut self, timeout: Duration) -> Result<usize, ReadyTimeoutError>` (lines 1114-1119)
- `ready_deadline(&mut self, deadline: Instant) -> Result<usize, ReadyTimeoutError>` (lines 1167-1172)

### `SelectedOperation` Completion (lines 1224-1319)

- `send<T>(self, s: &Sender<T>, msg: T) -> Result<(), SendError<T>>` - Complete send
- `recv<T>(self, r: &Receiver<T>) -> Result<T, RecvError>` - Complete recv

Both completion methods assert the address matches and call `unsafe { channel::write/read }`.

## 4. `select!` Macro Expansion

### Macro Structure (select_macro.rs)

The `crossbeam_channel_internal!` macro has two stages:

**Stage 1: Parsing** (lines 26-535)
- `@list` - tokenizes input into case list
- `@list_errorN` - diagnostics for syntax errors
- `@case` - validates individual case syntax

**Stage 2: Code Generation** (lines 537-992)

- Single recv → `recv()` (lines 559-571)

```rust

@add _sel (cases...) (default?) (labels...) (accumulated_cases...)
```

For each case, macro generates:
```rust
match $receiver_or_sender {
    ref _r => {
        let _operN: &Receiver/Sender<_> = unsafe { /* lifetime erasure */ };
        $sel[N] = (_operN, N, receiver_addr/sender_addr(_operN));

Three entry points based on default case:
1. **No default** (lines 755-775): Calls `internal::select(&mut $sel, _IS_BIASED)`
2. **default()** (lines 777-805): Calls `internal::try_select`, matches Option
3. **default(timeout)** (lines 807-835): Calls `internal::select_timeout`, matches Option

    let _res = $oper.recv($r0);  // or .send($s0, $msg)
    { $sel };  // drop sel early
    let $res = _res;
    $body0
} else if $oper.index() == 1 {
    // ...
} else {
    unreachable!()
}
```

- `select! { ... }` (lines 1136-1146): Sets `_IS_BIASED = false`
- `select_biased! { ... }` (lines 1157-1167): Sets `_IS_BIASED = true`

<result># Crossbeam-Channel Utilities, Examples, Tests, and Benchmarks

## 1. src/utils.rs

**Mutex wrapper**: Wraps `std::sync::Mutex<T>` in a `Mutex<T>` struct that automatically handles poisoning by calling `unwrap_or_else(PoisonError::into_inner)` on lock, providing a non-poisoning mutex interface.

**Cell and helpers**:
- Uses `Cell<Wrapping<u32>>` for thread-local RNG state (const-initialized with seed 1,406,868,647)
- **shuffle function**: Implements Fisher-Yates shuffle using a 32-bit Xorshift RNG (shifts 13, 17, 5) with Daniel Lemire's fast modulo reduction alternative
- **sleep_until function**: Sleeps until a deadline or forever if `None`, using `thread::sleep` with 1000-second default duration

No spin/yield logic is present in utils.rs.

## 2. src/alloc_helper.rs

Provides a `Global` allocator struct based on unstable `alloc::alloc::Global`, with key differences:
- Returns `NonNull<u8>` instead of `NonNull<[u8]>`
- Methods: `allocate()`, `allocate_zeroed()`, `deallocate()`
- Handles zero-sized allocations by returning dangling pointers aligned to the layout
- Uses `without_provenance_mut` function for creating pointers without provenance (uses `transmute` on Miri, `as` cast otherwise)

## 3. Examples

**fibonacci.rs**: Demonstrates an asynchronous fibonacci sequence generator using channels. Spawns a thread that repeatedly sends fibonacci numbers (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ...) into a bounded(0) channel until disconnected. Uses `Sender::send()`, `Receiver::iter()`, and `bounded()`.

**matching.rs**: Demonstrates using `select!` to send and receive on the same channel concurrently, based on a Go example. Multiple threads seek to either send their name or receive a peer's name using `select!` branches. Uses `bounded()`, `select!`, `Receiver::clone()`, and `try_recv()`. Demonstrates rendezvous communication patterns.

**stopwatch.rs**: Demonstrates a periodic timer with signal handling. Prints elapsed time every second using `tick()`, and exits on Ctrl+C using signal handling. Uses `select!` with multiple receivers, `tick()`, `bounded()`, and signal handling via `signal_hook`. Shows timeout-based selection with real-time updates.

## 4. Test Files (One-Line Characterization)

- **after.rs**: Tests for the `after` channel flavor (timed channels that fire after a deadline)
- **array.rs**: Tests for the array channel flavor (bounded channels with fixed capacity)
- **golang.rs**: Tests copied from Go's channel tests and manually rewritten in Rust
- **iter.rs**: Tests for iteration over receivers, including nested iteration and early break
- **list.rs**: Tests for the list channel flavor (unbounded channels)
- **mpsc.rs**: Tests copied from `std::sync::mpsc` standard library tests
- **never.rs**: Tests for the `never` channel flavor (channels that never produce values)
- **ready.rs**: Tests for channel readiness using the `Select` struct
- **same_channel.rs**: Tests for the `same_channel` method that identifies if senders/receivers belong to the same channel instance
- **select.rs**: Tests for channel selection using the `Select` struct (non-macro API)
- **select_macro.rs**: Tests for the `select!` and `select_biased!` macros
- **thread_locals.rs**: Tests that accessing thread-locals while exiting threads doesn't cause panics
- **tick.rs**: Tests for the `tick` channel flavor (periodic timer channels)
- **zero.rs**: Tests for the zero-capacity bounded channel flavor (rendezvous channels)

## 5. Benchmark Setup

**How benchmarks are run**:
- Script `run.sh` executes each contender and captures output to `*.txt` files
- Runs 5 main contenders: crossbeam-channel, futures-channel, mpsc (std), flume, Go
- Optional contenders commented out: atomicringqueue, bus, crossbeam-deque, lockfree, segqueue, mpmc
- Results plotted via Python script `plot.py`

**Benchmark types** (N=5,000,000 messages, T=4 threads):
- `seq`: Single thread sends N messages, then receives N messages
- `spsc`: One thread sends N messages, another receives N messages  
- `mpsc`: T threads send N/T messages each, one thread receives all
- `mpmc`: T threads send N/T messages each, T other threads receive N/T each
- `select_rx`: T threads send into separate channels, one thread selects over T channels
- `select_both`: T threads send via select over T channels, T threads receive via select over T channels

**Compared against**: Go channels, flume, futures-channel, std::sync::mpsc, with several optional lock-free implementations.</result>

# Crossbeam-Channel Utilities, Examples, Tests, and Benchmarks

## 1. src/utils.rs

**Mutex wrapper**: Wraps `std::sync::Mutex<T>` in a `Mutex<T>` struct that automatically handles poisoning by calling `unwrap_or_else(PoisonError::into_inner)` on lock, providing a non-poisoning mutex interface.

**Cell and helpers**:
- Uses `Cell<Wrapping<u32>>` for thread-local RNG state (const-initialized with seed 1,406,868,647)
- **shuffle function**: Implements Fisher-Yates shuffle using a 32-bit Xorshift RNG (shifts 13, 17, 5) with Daniel Lemire's fast modulo reduction alternative
- **sleep_until function**: Sleeps until a deadline or forever if `None`, using `thread::sleep` with 1000-second default duration

No spin/yield logic is present in utils.rs.

## 2. src/alloc_helper.rs

Provides a `Global` allocator struct based on unstable `alloc::alloc::Global`, with key differences:
- Returns `NonNull<u8>` instead of `NonNull<[u8]>`
- Methods: `allocate()`, `allocate_zeroed()`, `deallocate()`
- Handles zero-sized allocations by returning dangling pointers aligned to the layout
- Uses `without_provenance_mut` function for creating pointers without provenance (uses `transmute` on Miri, `as` cast otherwise)

## 3. Examples

**fibonacci.rs**: Demonstrates an asynchronous fibonacci sequence generator using channels. Spawns a thread that repeatedly sends fibonacci numbers (0, 1, 1, 2, 3, 5, ...) into a bounded(0) channel until disconnected. Uses `Sender::send()`, `Receiver::iter()`, and `bounded()`.

**matching.rs**: Demonstrates using `select!` to send and receive on the same channel concurrently, based on a Go example. Multiple threads seek to either send their name or receive a peer's name using `select!` branches. Uses `bounded()`, `select!`, `Receiver::clone()`, and `try_recv()`. Demonstrates rendezvous communication patterns.

**stopwatch.rs**: Demonstrates a periodic timer with signal handling. Prints elapsed time every second using `tick()`, and exits on Ctrl+C using signal handling. Uses `select!` with multiple receivers, `tick()`, `bounded()`, and signal handling via `signal_hook`. Shows timeout-based selection with real-time updates.

## 4. Test Files (One-Line Characterization)

- **after.rs**: Tests for the `after` channel flavor (timed channels that fire after a deadline)
- **array.rs**: Tests for the array channel flavor (bounded channels with fixed capacity)
- **golang.rs**: Tests copied from Go's channel tests and manually rewritten in Rust
- **iter.rs**: Tests for iteration over receivers, including nested iteration and early break
- **list.rs**: Tests for the list channel flavor (unbounded channels)
- **mpsc.rs**: Tests copied from `std::sync::mpsc` standard library tests
- **never.rs**: Tests for the `never` channel flavor (channels that never produce values)
- **ready.rs**: Tests for channel readiness using the `Select` struct
- **same_channel.rs**: Tests for the `same_channel` method that identifies if senders/receivers belong to the same channel instance
- **select.rs**: Tests for channel selection using the `Select` struct (non-macro API)
- **select_macro.rs**: Tests for the `select!` and `select_biased!` macros
- **thread_locals.rs**: Tests that accessing thread-locals while exiting threads doesn't cause panics
- **tick.rs**: Tests for the `tick` channel flavor (periodic timer channels)
- **zero.rs**: Tests for the zero-capacity bounded channel flavor (rendezvous channels)

## 5. Benchmark Setup

**How benchmarks are run**:
- Script `run.sh` executes each contender and captures output to `*.txt` files
- Runs 5 main contenders: crossbeam-channel, futures-channel, mpsc (std), flume, Go
- Optional contenders commented out: atomicringqueue, bus, crossbeam-deque, lockfree, segqueue, mpmc
- Results plotted via Python script `plot.py`

**Benchmark types** (N=5,000,000 messages, T=4 threads):
- `seq`: Single thread sends N messages, then receives N messages
- `spsc`: One thread sends N messages, another receives N messages  
- `mpsc`: T threads send N/T messages each, one thread receives all
- `mpmc`: T threads send N/T messages each, T other threads receive N/T each
- `select_rx`: T threads send into separate channels, one thread selects over T channels
- `select_both`: T threads send via select over T channels, T threads receive via select over T channels

**Compared against**: Go channels, flume, futures-channel, std::sync::mpsc, with several optional lock-free implementations.
